title: Barrett's Oesophagus
oneLiner: Columnar metaplasia of distal oesophagus due to GERD; increases risk of oesophageal adenocarcinoma by ~0.5%/year; surveillance is key.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Oesophageal adenocarcinoma with perforation:** sudden onset severe chest/back pain, subcutaneous emphysema, shock — do CXR (free air under diaphragm) before any PPI.
- **Oesophageal perforation (Boerhaave / iatrogenic):** severe vomiting or recent endoscopy + surgical emphysema + mediastinal crackle (Hamman’s sign) — rule out before treating reflux.
- **Myocardial infarction (MI) mimicking oesophageal spasm:** if chest pain + haemodynamic instability, get ECG + troponin before giving antacids.
sections.Presentation:
- Usually asymptomatic – incidental finding on endoscopy for dyspepsia/GORD
- **Chronic GORD symptoms:** heartburn, regurgitation, dysphagia, chronic cough
- **Alarm symptoms:** dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, haematemesis, melena (suggest advanced disease/cancer)
- **Key exam findings:** usually none; look for peripheral stigmata of anaemia, lymphadenopathy (supraclavicular → Virchow’s node suggests mets)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **GORD (no Barrett’s):** symptoms same; only endoscopy with biopsy can differentiate.
- **Oesophagitis:** erosions on endoscopy; no intestinal metaplasia on histology.
- **Oesophageal motility disorder:** dysphagia for solids + liquids; manometry diagnostic.
- **Oesophageal carcinoma:** dysphagia progressive, weight loss; visible mass on EGD ± CT.
- **Peptic ulcer disease:** epigastric pain, relieved by food; H. pylori testing.
- **Functional dyspepsia:** no alarm features; normal endoscopy.
- **Splitter:** **Prague classification C and M** (circumferential and maximal extent) on endoscopy — Barrett’s if salmon-coloured mucosa >1 cm above GOJ; histology required to confirm intestinal metaplasia.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** ECG + troponin if chest pain; assess vitals; check for anaemia (conjunctival pallor).
- **Bloods:** FBC (anaemia), iron studies (chronic blood loss), LFTs (may be normal unless mets). No specific biomarker.
- **Imaging:** 
  - **First-line:** Upper GI endoscopy (OGD) with systematic biopsies (Seattle protocol — 4-quadrant q2cm in Barrett’s segment; visible lesions targeted).
  - **Second-line:** EUS if high-grade dysplasia (HGD) or suspected cancer; CT chest/abdomen if cancer.
- **Special tests:** Histology from biopsies (intestinal metaplasia ± dysplasia). Consider pH monitoring if GERD symptoms refractory.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Prague classification (C & M extent); **criteria for Barrett’s:** endoscopically suspected oesophageal metaplasia (ESEM) + histologic intestinal metaplasia (goblet cells).
- **Never-miss test:** **Histology from systematic biopsies** — omitting biopsies or taking insufficient sample misses dysplasia/cancer.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** 
  - If acute GI bleed (haematemesis/melena) → resuscitate (IV fluids, cross-match), urgent OGD; if perforation suspected → NBM, IV antibiotics, CT, surgical referral.
  - If no acute issue → reassure, initiate/long-term acid suppression.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** 
  - For new diagnosis on endoscopy: confirm histology, assess Prague extent, repeat OGD with biopsies if initial inadequate.
  - Start PPI (e.g., omeprazole 20mg once daily) for symptom control and potential regression of acid exposure.
  - Refer to upper GI multidisciplinary team if dysplasia or cancer found.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Surveillance endoscopy** (NICE guideline 2019):
    - No dysplasia: repeat OGD every 3–5 years (if segment length <3cm and two consecutive OGDs benign, consider discharge).
    - Low-grade dysplasia (LGD): repeat OGD with biopsies within 6 months; if confirmed, consider endoscopic therapy (RF ablation) or annual surveillance.
    - High-grade dysplasia (HGD): urgent referral for endoscopic eradication therapy (RF ablation, EMR) or surgery if visible lesion.
  - Lifestyle: weight loss, elevation of head of bed, avoid trigger foods, smoking cessation.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reason):** 
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride if NBM; otherwise oral diet as tolerated.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) if no contraindication.
  - **Diet:** NBM only if acute bleed/perforation; otherwise regular diet.
  - **Observations:** baseline NEWS2, hourly if unstable; then 4-hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line PPI:** Omeprazole 20mg once daily PO (or Lansoprazole 30mg OD, Pantoprazole 40mg OD). For severe symptoms: omeprazole 40mg OD or BD.
- **Duration:** Long-term for symptom control and to reduce cancer risk (weak evidence). Typically trial 8 weeks then reassess.
- **Alternatives:** Esomeprazole 20mg OD, Rabeprazole 20mg OD.
- **Key contraindications:** hypomagnesaemia (check Mg if long-term), increased risk of *Clostridium difficile*.
- **Interactions:** reduces absorption of itraconazole, ketoconazole, atazanavir; increases risk of methotrexate toxicity; may potentiate warfarin (minor).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** no routine dose adjustment for mild–moderate renal impairment; caution in severe hepatic impairment (reduce dose or space interval — [check local policy]).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing PPI without checking for *C. diff* or hypomagnesaemia in elderly** — long-term PPI increases fracture risk, *C. diff*, and Mg deficiency.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** 
  - GI bleeding from erosive oesophagitis or cancer (rare as first presentation).
  - Oesophageal perforation (endoscopy related – risk 1:10,000; higher if EMR/EUS).
- **Chronic:** 
  - **Dysplasia progression:** low-grade → high-grade → adenocarcinoma (annual risk ~0.5%).
  - **Oesophageal stricture:** dysphagia from chronic reflux.
  - **Anaemia:** from chronic blood loss (iron deficiency).
  - **Extra-oesophageal:** chronic cough, asthma exacerbation, laryngitis.
sections.Prognosis:
- **Without dysplasia:** excellent — cancer risk ~0.5% per year; survival near normal.
- **With high-grade dysplasia:** if treated (RFA/EMR), progression to cancer <5%; if untreated, 30% develop adenocarcinoma within 5 years.
- **Oesophageal adenocarcinoma:** 5-year survival <20% if symptomatic; >80% if detected in Barrett’s surveillance at early stage.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always document Prague classification (C and M) in the endoscopy report** — this guides surveillance interval.
- **If you see Barrett’s on OGD, take systematic 4-quadrant biopsies every 2cm** — single random biopsy misses 50% of dysplasia.
- **For any patient with dysphagia and known Barrett’s, refer for urgent OGD ± CT** — could be a stricture or cancer.
- **Start PPI even if asymptomatic** — reduces acid reflux and inflammation, may lower risk of dysplasia.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT skip biopsies because the segment looks “short”** — even <1cm Barrett’s can harbour dysplasia.
- **Do NOT discharge a patient with Barrett’s without arranging surveillance** — missed cancer is a common medicolegal claim.
- **Do NOT ignore weight loss + dysphagia as “simple reflux”** — always consider cancer until proven otherwise.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** 
  - Dysphagia (new or worsening)
  - Weight loss >5% unintentional
  - Haematemesis or melena
  - Severe chest pain that is worse with swallowing
- **Follow-up:** 
  - Refer to GI outpatient for surveillance OGD within 6 months if dysplastic; otherwise schedule as per histology result.
  - Ensure patient receives a written copy of endoscopy findings and next planned date.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L
  - Suspected oesophageal perforation (any crepitus, fever, shock)
  - Haematemesis with instability (sBP <100, HR >100)
  - New onset dysphagia with weight loss
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Requirement for vasopressors or respiratory support
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rising lactate >4 despite resuscitation
- **Crash team if:**
  - Cardiorespiratory arrest
  - Agonal breathing / unresponsive

rawMarkdown:
## Barrett's Oesophagus
**One-liner:** Columnar metaplasia of distal oesophagus due to GERD; increases risk of oesophageal adenocarcinoma by ~0.5%/year; surveillance is key.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Oesophageal adenocarcinoma with perforation:** sudden onset severe chest/back pain, subcutaneous emphysema, shock — do CXR (free air under diaphragm) before any PPI.
- **Oesophageal perforation (Boerhaave / iatrogenic):** severe vomiting or recent endoscopy + surgical emphysema + mediastinal crackle (Hamman’s sign) — rule out before treating reflux.
- **Myocardial infarction (MI) mimicking oesophageal spasm:** if chest pain + haemodynamic instability, get ECG + troponin before giving antacids.

### Presentation
- Usually asymptomatic – incidental finding on endoscopy for dyspepsia/GORD
- **Chronic GORD symptoms:** heartburn, regurgitation, dysphagia, chronic cough
- **Alarm symptoms:** dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, haematemesis, melena (suggest advanced disease/cancer)
- **Key exam findings:** usually none; look for peripheral stigmata of anaemia, lymphadenopathy (supraclavicular → Virchow’s node suggests mets)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **GORD (no Barrett’s):** symptoms same; only endoscopy with biopsy can differentiate.
- **Oesophagitis:** erosions on endoscopy; no intestinal metaplasia on histology.
- **Oesophageal motility disorder:** dysphagia for solids + liquids; manometry diagnostic.
- **Oesophageal carcinoma:** dysphagia progressive, weight loss; visible mass on EGD ± CT.
- **Peptic ulcer disease:** epigastric pain, relieved by food; H. pylori testing.
- **Functional dyspepsia:** no alarm features; normal endoscopy.
- **Splitter:** **Prague classification C and M** (circumferential and maximal extent) on endoscopy — Barrett’s if salmon-coloured mucosa >1 cm above GOJ; histology required to confirm intestinal metaplasia.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** ECG + troponin if chest pain; assess vitals; check for anaemia (conjunctival pallor).
- **Bloods:** FBC (anaemia), iron studies (chronic blood loss), LFTs (may be normal unless mets). No specific biomarker.
- **Imaging:** 
  - **First-line:** Upper GI endoscopy (OGD) with systematic biopsies (Seattle protocol — 4-quadrant q2cm in Barrett’s segment; visible lesions targeted).
  - **Second-line:** EUS if high-grade dysplasia (HGD) or suspected cancer; CT chest/abdomen if cancer.
- **Special tests:** Histology from biopsies (intestinal metaplasia ± dysplasia). Consider pH monitoring if GERD symptoms refractory.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Prague classification (C & M extent); **criteria for Barrett’s:** endoscopically suspected oesophageal metaplasia (ESEM) + histologic intestinal metaplasia (goblet cells).
- **Never-miss test:** **Histology from systematic biopsies** — omitting biopsies or taking insufficient sample misses dysplasia/cancer.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** 
  - If acute GI bleed (haematemesis/melena) → resuscitate (IV fluids, cross-match), urgent OGD; if perforation suspected → NBM, IV antibiotics, CT, surgical referral.
  - If no acute issue → reassure, initiate/long-term acid suppression.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** 
  - For new diagnosis on endoscopy: confirm histology, assess Prague extent, repeat OGD with biopsies if initial inadequate.
  - Start PPI (e.g., omeprazole 20mg once daily) for symptom control and potential regression of acid exposure.
  - Refer to upper GI multidisciplinary team if dysplasia or cancer found.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Surveillance endoscopy** (NICE guideline 2019):
    - No dysplasia: repeat OGD every 3–5 years (if segment length <3cm and two consecutive OGDs benign, consider discharge).
    - Low-grade dysplasia (LGD): repeat OGD with biopsies within 6 months; if confirmed, consider endoscopic therapy (RF ablation) or annual surveillance.
    - High-grade dysplasia (HGD): urgent referral for endoscopic eradication therapy (RF ablation, EMR) or surgery if visible lesion.
  - Lifestyle: weight loss, elevation of head of bed, avoid trigger foods, smoking cessation.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reason):** 
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride if NBM; otherwise oral diet as tolerated.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) if no contraindication.
  - **Diet:** NBM only if acute bleed/perforation; otherwise regular diet.
  - **Observations:** baseline NEWS2, hourly if unstable; then 4-hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line PPI:** Omeprazole 20mg once daily PO (or Lansoprazole 30mg OD, Pantoprazole 40mg OD). For severe symptoms: omeprazole 40mg OD or BD.
- **Duration:** Long-term for symptom control and to reduce cancer risk (weak evidence). Typically trial 8 weeks then reassess.
- **Alternatives:** Esomeprazole 20mg OD, Rabeprazole 20mg OD.
- **Key contraindications:** hypomagnesaemia (check Mg if long-term), increased risk of *Clostridium difficile*.
- **Interactions:** reduces absorption of itraconazole, ketoconazole, atazanavir; increases risk of methotrexate toxicity; may potentiate warfarin (minor).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** no routine dose adjustment for mild–moderate renal impairment; caution in severe hepatic impairment (reduce dose or space interval — [check local policy]).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing PPI without checking for *C. diff* or hypomagnesaemia in elderly** — long-term PPI increases fracture risk, *C. diff*, and Mg deficiency.

### Complications
- **Acute:** 
  - GI bleeding from erosive oesophagitis or cancer (rare as first presentation).
  - Oesophageal perforation (endoscopy related – risk 1:10,000; higher if EMR/EUS).
- **Chronic:** 
  - **Dysplasia progression:** low-grade → high-grade → adenocarcinoma (annual risk ~0.5%).
  - **Oesophageal stricture:** dysphagia from chronic reflux.
  - **Anaemia:** from chronic blood loss (iron deficiency).
  - **Extra-oesophageal:** chronic cough, asthma exacerbation, laryngitis.

### Prognosis
- **Without dysplasia:** excellent — cancer risk ~0.5% per year; survival near normal.
- **With high-grade dysplasia:** if treated (RFA/EMR), progression to cancer <5%; if untreated, 30% develop adenocarcinoma within 5 years.
- **Oesophageal adenocarcinoma:** 5-year survival <20% if symptomatic; >80% if detected in Barrett’s surveillance at early stage.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always document Prague classification (C and M) in the endoscopy report** — this guides surveillance interval.
- **If you see Barrett’s on OGD, take systematic 4-quadrant biopsies every 2cm** — single random biopsy misses 50% of dysplasia.
- **For any patient with dysphagia and known Barrett’s, refer for urgent OGD ± CT** — could be a stricture or cancer.
- **Start PPI even if asymptomatic** — reduces acid reflux and inflammation, may lower risk of dysplasia.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT skip biopsies because the segment looks “short”** — even <1cm Barrett’s can harbour dysplasia.
- **Do NOT discharge a patient with Barrett’s without arranging surveillance** — missed cancer is a common medicolegal claim.
- **Do NOT ignore weight loss + dysphagia as “simple reflux”** — always consider cancer until proven otherwise.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** 
  - Dysphagia (new or worsening)
  - Weight loss >5% unintentional
  - Haematemesis or melena
  - Severe chest pain that is worse with swallowing
- **Follow-up:** 
  - Refer to GI outpatient for surveillance OGD within 6 months if dysplastic; otherwise schedule as per histology result.
  - Ensure patient receives a written copy of endoscopy findings and next planned date.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L
  - Suspected oesophageal perforation (any crepitus, fever, shock)
  - Haematemesis with instability (sBP <100, HR >100)
  - New onset dysphagia with weight loss
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Requirement for vasopressors or respiratory support
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rising lactate >4 despite resuscitation
- **Crash team if:**
  - Cardiorespiratory arrest
  - Agonal breathing / unresponsive
